raw_text: REPORTABLE IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION CIVIL APPEAL Nos.823-854 OF 2016 (Arising out of SLP (C ) Nos. 15919- 15950 of 2011) Kerala Public Service Commission  Ors. ..Appellants Versus The State Information Commission  Anr. .Respondents With CIVIL APPEAL NO.855 OF 2016 (Arising out of SLP (Civil) No.5433 of 2014) Public Service Commission U.P. ..Appellant Versus Raghvendra Singh .. Respondent J U D G M E N T M.Y. EQBAL, J. Leave granted. 2. In these two appeals the short question which needs consideration is as to whether the Division Bench of the Kerala High Court by impugned judgment has right ly held that the respondents are entitled not only to get information with regard to the scan copies of their answer sheet, 1 561 Page 1 tabulation-sheet containing interview marks but also entitled to know the names of the examiners who have evaluate d the answer sheet. 3. The information sought for by the respondents were denied by the State Public Information Officer and the Appellate Authority. However, the State Information Commission allowed the second appeal and held that there is no fiduciary rela tionship in case of answer scripts. Further, the interview marks cannot be considered as personal information, since the public authority had already decided to publish them. 4. Both the High Courts of Kerala and Allahabad have taken the view, following th e earlier decisions of this Court that no fiduciary relationship exists between the appellants and the respondents and, therefore, the information sought for have to be supplied to them. 5. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and have gone through the impugned judgments passed by the 2 562 Page 2 Division Bench of the High Court of Kerala at Ernakulam and Allahabad. 6. So far as the information sought for by the respondents with regard to the supply of scanned copies of his answer -sheet of the written test, copy of the tabulation sheet and other information, we are of the opinion that the view taken in the impugned judgment with regard to the disclosure of these information, do not suffer from error of law and the same is fully justified. However, the view of the Kerala High Court is that the information seekers are also entitled to get the disclosure of names of examiners who have evaluated the answer-sheet. 7. The view taken by the Kerala High Court holding that no fiduciary relationship exists b etween the University and the Commission and the examiners appointed by them cannot be sustained in law. The Kerala High Court while observing held:- 16.What, if any , is the fiduciary relationship of the PSC qua the examinees? Performance audit of constitutional institutions would only 3 563 Page 3 strengthen the confidence of the citizenry in such institutions. The PSC is a constitutional institution. To stand above board, is one of its own prime requirements. There is nothing that should deter disclosure of the contents of the materials that the examinees provide as part of their performance in the competition for being selected to public service. The confidence that may be reposed by the examinees in the institution of the PSC does not inspire the acceptability of a fiduciary relationship that should kindle the exclusion of information in relation to the evalution or other details relating to the examination. Once the evaluation is over and results are declared, no more secrecy is called for. D issemination of such information would only add to the credibility of the PSC, in the constitutional conspectus in which it is placed. A particular examinee would therefore be entitled to access to information in relation to that persons answer scripts. A s regards others, information in relation to answer scripts may fall within the pale of third party information in terms of section 11 of the RTI Act. This only means that such information cannot be accessed except in conformity with the provisions conta ined in section 11. It does not, in any manner, provide for any immunity from access. 17. We shall now examine the next contention of PSC that there is a fiduciary relationship between it and the examiners and as a consequence, it is eligible to claim pro tection from disclosure, except with the sanction of the competent authority, as regards the identity of the examiners as also the materials that were subjected to the examination. We have already approved TREESA and the different precedents and commentari es relied on therein as regards the concept of fiduciary relationship. We are in full agreement with the law laid by the Division Bench of this Court in Centre of Earth Science 4 564 Page 4 Studies (supra), that S.8 (1)(e) deals with information available with the person in his fiduciary relationship with another; that information under this head is nothing but information in trust, which, but for the relationship would not have been conveyed or known to the person concerned. What is it that the PS C holds in trust for the examiners? Nothing. At the best, it could be pointed out that the identity of the examiners has to be insulated from public gaze, having regard to issues relatable to vulnerability and exposure to corruption if the identities of th e examiners are disclosed in advance. But, at any rate, such issues would go to oblivion after the conclusion of the evaluation of the answer scripts and the publication of the results. Therefore, it would not be in public interest to hold that there could be a continued secrecy even as regards the identity of the examiners. Access to such information, including as to the identity of the examiners, after the examination and evaluation process are over, cannot be shied off under any law or avowed principle of privacy. 8. We do not find any substance in the reasoning given by the Kerala High Court on the question of disclosure of names of the examiners. 9. In the present case, the PSC has taken upon itself in appointing the examiners to evaluate the answer papers and as such, the PSC and examiners stand in a principal -agent relationship. Here the PSC in the shoes of a Principal 5 565 Page 5 has entrusted the task of evaluating the answer papers to the Examiners. Consequently, Examiners in the positio n of agents are bound to evaluate the answer papers as per the instructions given by the PSC. As a result, a fiduciary relationship is established between the PSC and the Examiners. Therefore, any information shared between them is not liable to be disclos ed. Furthermore, the information seeker has no role to play in this and we dont see any logical reason as to how this will benefit him or the public at large. We would like to point out that the disclosure of the identity of Examiners is in the least inte rest of the general public and also any attempt to reveal the examiners identity will give rise to dire consequences. Therefore, in our considered opinion revealing examiners identity will only lead to confusion and public unrest. Hence, we are not inclined to agree with the decision of the Kerala High Court with respect to the second question. 6 566 Page 6 10. In the present case the request of the information seeker about the information of his answer sheets and details of the interview marks can be and should be provided to him. It is not something which a public authority keeps it under a fiduciary capacity. Even disclosing the marks and the answer sheets to the candidates will ensure that the candidates have been given marks according to their performance in the exam. This practice will ensure a fair play in this competitive environment, where candidate puts his time in preparing for the competitive exams, but, the request of the information seeker about the details of the person who had examinedchecked the paper cannot and shall not be provided to the information seeker as the relationship between the public authority i.e. Service Commission and the Examiners is totally within fiduciary relationship. The Commission has reposed trust on t he examiners that they will check the exam papers with utmost care, honesty and impartially and, similarly, the Examiners have faith that 7 567 Page 7 they will not be facing any unfortunate consequences for doing their job properly. If we allo w disclosing name of the examiners in every exam, the unsuccessful candidates may try to take revenge from the examiners for doing their job properly. This may, further, create a situation where the potential candidates in the next similar exam, especially in the same state or in the same level will try to contact the disclosed examiners for any potential gain by illegal means in the potential exam. 11. We, therefore, allow these appeals in part and modify the judgment only to the extent that the respondents -applicants are not entitled to the disclosure of names of the examiners as sought for by them. .J. (M.Y. Eqbal) .J. (Arun Mishra) New Delhi February 4, 2016 8 568 Page 8

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Honorable Court, I stand before you today to represent the plaintiff in this matter, seeking to establish the transparency and accountability of the examination process. The defendant's argument that disclosing the identities of examiners would lead to vulnerability and exposure to corruption is unfounded and lacks merit. In fact, the Kerala High Court has already observed that no fiduciary relationship exists between the university and the commission, and the examiners appointed by them cannot be sustained in law.

I would like to cite the landmark case of S.P. Gupta vs. Union of India (1981), where the Supreme Court held that the right to information is an integral part of the right to freedom of speech and expression under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution. In this case, the plaintiff has a right to know the identities of the examiners to ensure fairness and transparency in the evaluation process.

Furthermore, Section 4 of the Right to Information Act, 2005, mandates that all public authorities shall provide reasons for their decisions and make their processes transparent. By not disclosing the identities of the examiners, the defendant is violating this provision and undermining the trust of the citizens in the examination process. I urge the Court to consider the public interest in promoting transparency and accountability in the examination process, and I am confident that the plaintiff's case will prevail. I request the Court to allow me to proceed with my arguments and establish the liability and damages suffered by the plaintiff.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
My Lord, I strongly disagree with the plaintiff's claims. The issue at hand is not about creating a continued secrecy, but rather about protecting the integrity of the examination process. Disclosing the identities of examiners in advance would indeed expose them to corruption and vulnerability, which could compromise the fairness of the evaluation. The fact that such issues may become obsolete after the publication of results is irrelevant, as the damage would already be done.

I would like to cite the landmark case of Maharashtra State Board of Secondary and Higher Secondary Education vs. K.S. Gandhi (2005), where the Supreme Court held that the right to privacy is an integral part of the right to life under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution. In this context, the examiners' right to privacy and protection from potential harassment or coercion is paramount.

Furthermore, I challenge the plaintiff's assumption that no fiduciary relationship exists between the university and the commission. Section 3 of the Kerala Public Service Commission Act, 1976, clearly establishes a fiduciary relationship between the commission and the examinees. I request the court to reconsider the Kerala High Court's observation in light of this statutory provision.

I also question the credibility of the plaintiff's evidence, which fails to demonstrate any concrete harm or prejudice caused by the non-disclosure of examiners' identities. In the absence of such evidence, I urge the court to exercise caution and prioritize the integrity of the examination process.

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Your Honor, I stand before this court today to represent the plaintiff in a matter of great importance, where the integrity of the examination process and the rights of the examinees are at stake. The defendant's argument that disclosing the identities of examiners in advance would lead to corruption and vulnerability is unfounded and contrary to the principles of transparency and accountability.

I would like to cite the landmark judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of S.P. Gupta vs. President of India, (1981) 1 SCC 87, which held that the right to information is a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution. In this case, the plaintiff has a legitimate right to know the identities of the examiners who evaluated their answer scripts.

Furthermore, the Kerala High Court's observation that there is no fiduciary relationship between the university and the commission, and the examiners appointed by them, is a significant precedent in this matter. This establishes that the examiners are not in a position of trust or confidence, and therefore, their identities should not be kept secret.

I will demonstrate that the defendant's actions have caused harm and prejudice to the plaintiff, and that the court should exercise its jurisdiction to ensure transparency and fairness in the examination process. I will also address the defendant's potential counterarguments and establish that the plaintiff is entitled to relief. I request the court to consider the evidence and arguments presented, and to deliver a verdict that upholds the principles of transparency, accountability, and fairness.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Your Honor, I strongly argue that the disclosure of examiners' identities would indeed create a vulnerability to corruption and undermine the integrity of the examination process. The fact that such issues may become obsolete after the evaluation and publication of results is irrelevant, as the damage would already be done. The plaintiff's claim that no fiduciary relationship exists between the university, commission, and examiners is misconstrued. 

As per the Indian Contract Act, 1872, a fiduciary relationship is established when one party is in a position of trust and confidence with respect to another. In this case, the examiners, appointed by the university and commission, owe a duty of confidentiality and impartiality to the examinees. Disclosing their identities would compromise this fiduciary relationship and potentially lead to undue influence or bias.

I cite the landmark case of State of Kerala vs. Kerala Public Service Commission (2006), where the Kerala High Court held that the Public Service Commission has a fiduciary duty to maintain the confidentiality of its examination process. Furthermore, Section 8(1)(j) of the Right to Information Act, 2005, exempts information that would compromise the confidentiality of examination processes. I urge the court to consider the potential consequences of disclosing examiners' identities and uphold the defendant's right to maintain confidentiality, thereby ensuring the integrity of the examination process.

reasoning: The plaintiff's right to information and transparency in the examination process is protected under Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution, as established in the landmark case of S.P. Gupta vs. Union of India (1981).

The Kerala High Court's observation that no fiduciary relationship exists between the university and the commission, and the examiners appointed by them, supports the plaintiff's claim for transparency and accountability in the examination process.

The defendant's argument that disclosing examiners' identities would lead to corruption and vulnerability is not supported by concrete evidence, and the potential harm can be mitigated through other means, such as anonymizing the evaluation process.
supporting_evidence: The landmark case of S.P. Gupta vs. Union of India (1981), which established the right to information as an integral part of the right to freedom of speech and expression under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution.
The Kerala High Court's observation that no fiduciary relationship exists between the university and the commission, and the examiners appointed by them, as mentioned in the case facts.
Section 4 of the Right to Information Act, 2005, which mandates that all public authorities shall provide reasons for their decisions and make their processes transparent.

recommendations: The judge's verdict should explicitly address potential biases and assumptions in the defendant's argument.
Future cases should consider anonymizing the evaluation process to mitigate potential corruption and vulnerability.
The court should ensure that all parties have equal access to information and opportunities to present their claims.
audit.summary: The verdict appears to be fair and unbiased, with the judge relying on established case law and logical reasoning. However, the lack of clear case facts and the potential for implicit biases in the defendant's argument warrant a score of 90 rather than 100. No explicit gender-based language, regional/caste/religion proxies, or discriminatory language were detected.
